g.data-narration: Welcome to this lecture on Encapsulating Security Payload, the cornerstone protocol for secure network communication.
ESP is an IPSec protocol that provides confidentiality, data integrity, authentication, and anti-replay protection for IP packets.
Unlike AH which only provides authentication, ESP encrypts the payload while optionally providing authentication, making it preferred for most IPSec implementations.
g.text: ESP vs AH - Why Choose ESP?

g.data-narration: The ESP header contains the SPI - a 32-bit identifier that helps lookup the Security Association for processing each packet.
The ESP trailer contains padding for block alignment, the next header field, and optional authentication data for message integrity verification.
In Transport mode, ESP encrypts only the payload (TCP header and data) while preserving the original IP header for end-to-end security.
g.text: Encrypts only payload • End-to-end security between hosts

g.data-narration: In Tunnel mode, ESP encrypts the entire original IP packet and adds a new IP header, used for site-to-site VPNs between gateways.
g.text: Encrypts entire IP packet • Site-to-site VPNs between gateways

g.data-narration: Transport mode preserves the original IP header for host-to-host communication, while tunnel mode creates a new IP header for network-to-network VPN connections.
g.text: • Adds new IP header for routing

g.data-narration: ESP supports various encryption algorithms like AES and 3DES for confidentiality, and HMAC-SHA for authentication and data integrity verification.
g.text: 💡 ESP can use combined mode algorithms like AES-GCM that provide both encryption and authentication

g.data-narration: ESP integrates with IKE (Internet Key Exchange) for automatic Security Association establishment and key management using Diffie-Hellman exchange.
g.text: Internet Key Exchange manages SA establishment and cryptographic keys

g.data-narration: Outbound ESP processing: lookup SA, generate sequence number, encrypt payload, calculate ICV for authentication, then add ESP header and trailer.
Inbound ESP processing validates the sequence number for anti-replay, verifies authentication, decrypts the payload, then passes data to upper layers.
Example: Host A (192.168.1.10) sends encrypted packet to Host B using AES-256 encryption and HMAC-SHA256 authentication.
Important formulas: padding calculation ensures block alignment, HMAC provides authentication, and total ESP packet length includes all components.
Key takeaways: ESP provides encryption + optional authentication, uses SPI for SA lookup, sequence numbers prevent replay attacks, and IKE manages key exchange.
